summary: Mixed show with solid Eyes & Jack Straw, vocal slip‑ups dominate
blurb: The set offers a few bright spots—Jerry’s guitar shines on Eyes Of The World and the band nails It Must Have Been The Roses—but sloppy vocals and an infamous Get Back drag the overall quality. Highlights include Jack Straw, When Push Comes To Shove and Black Peter, though the second‑set flow feels uneven.
review: On January 28 1987 at the San Francisco Civic Auditorium the Dead delivered a performance that swings between moments of brilliance and conspicuous missteps. The first set contains gems like It Must Have Been The Roses, Row Jimmy and a quirky Jack Straw, with Jerry’s lead guitar cutting through even as his vocals wobble on Get Back. The second set is more erratic: While the drum‑to‑Eyes transition dazzles and Brent’s keyboards keep things anchored, the vocal delivery remains uneven, especially on the Beatles cover and several lyrical flubs. Overall, the night is a snapshot of the band’s post‑coma turbulence—worth listening for its highlights but not a classic show.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Multiple recordings repeatedly note vocal slip‑ups, off‑key singing and a generally sloppy performance (e.g., Get Back, overall set disjointedness). Positive remarks are limited to isolated songs and drumming highlights, so the consensus leans negative on show quality.

best_recording.reason: Crystal‑clear soundboard transfer captures the full show with no dropouts, making it the most complete and sonically reliable source despite the performance flaws.

key_highlights: Jerry’s lead guitar shines on Eyes Of The World and Jack Straw.
Brent’s keyboards hold the set together, especially during the drum‑to‑Eyes transition.
Strong moments: It Must Have Been The Roses, When Push Comes To Shove, Black Peter.
Vocal issues dominate, notably a botched Get Back that drags down the overall flow.
song_highlights: It Must Have Been The Roses

band_performance.Jerry: Vocals are frequently off‑key and slurred (especially on Get Back), but his guitar work remains a highlight, delivering strong solos on Eyes Of The World, Jack Straw, and the closing of It Must Have Been The Roses.
band_performance.Phil: Bass is solid and surprisingly audible; several reviewers note steady playing and occasional standout solo moments in the matrix mixes.
band_performance.Bob: Vocal performance is strained and often out of tune, with lyric flubs on Get Back and other songs, reducing his overall impact despite his rhythmic contributions.
band_performance.Brent: Keyboard work is a saving grace, providing consistent support and praised for solid playing on Eyes Of The World and Jack Straw; generally keeps the band grounded amid vocal mishaps.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums are energetic and jam well, highlighted by a memorable drum‑to‑Eyes transition that lifts the set briefly.
band_performance.Billy: Provides a steady rhythmic foundation; drumming is competent throughout, helping maintain momentum despite vocal inconsistencies.

must_listen_sequences: It Must Have Been The Roses
